order_type__stat_request.stat_request_reason: Patient has experienced three vaso-occlusive crises requiring emergency department visits in the last six months despite adherence to hydroxyurea and optimized supportive care. He has had increasing frequency of pain episodes interfering with work and daily functioning, with two missed weeks of work in the past two months. Given ongoing high utilization of acute care, uncontrolled vaso-occlusive pain, and risk for further complications, provider is requesting STAT initiation of ADAKVEO to reduce frequency of VOCs and prevent further organ damage. Rapid approval is requested to avoid another anticipated VOC during the upcoming winter season, when his crises historically worsen.

patient_information.name: Marcus DeShawn Carter
patient_information.address: 1842 S Peoria Ave, Apartment 3B, Tulsa, OK 74120
patient_information.allergies: Allergic to morphine (pruritus and hives after IV morphine in ED in 2019, switched to hydromorphone with good tolerance). Mild seasonal allergies (ragweed and grass pollen) causing sneezing and itchy eyes in late spring and early fall; managed with cetirizine as needed. No known allergies to foods, latex, or contrast dye. No known prior reactions to monoclonal antibody therapies or IV infusions.

adakveo_order_details.adakveo_order_selection: Initial/Reloading Dosing and then Maintenance Dosing

diagnosis__stat_reason.stat_reason:
26-year-old man with homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) and recurrent vaso-occlusive crises despite adherence to hydroxyurea at maximally tolerated dose and regular follow-up. In the past 6 months he has had three ED visits and one 4-day hospitalization for VOC-related pain (most recently 2025-11-18), each requiring IV opioids and hydration. He reports near-weekly moderate pain episodes at home that interfere with sleep and make it difficult to maintain full-time employment as a warehouse inventory associate, especially with prolonged standing and lifting.

He has early evidence of end-organ involvement, including mild microalbuminuria and intermittent exertional dyspnea. Winter months historically precipitate more frequent and severe VOCs for him due to cold exposure and increased respiratory infections. Given his current clinical trajectory, ongoing health-care utilization, and impact on quality of life and functional status, provider is requesting STAT initiation of ADAKVEO to reduce VOC frequency and intensity. Rapid approval is medically necessary to prevent another likely hospitalization in the next 1–2 months and to stabilize his condition before peak winter season. Delay in therapy initiation may lead to further organ damage, increased opioid exposure with associated risks, and additional missed work days, potentially jeopardizing his employment and insurance coverage.

infusion__lab_orders.lab_frequency: Obtain CBC with differential and CMP prior to first ADAKVEO infusion (baseline), then every 3 months thereafter while on therapy, or more frequently if clinically indicated (e.g., new symptoms, change in clinical status, or abnormal prior results).

notes__additional_comments.notes_additional_comments:
Marcus is a 33-year-old male with homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) who recently relocated to Tulsa from Oklahoma City to be closer to family and for more stable employment. He works full-time as an inventory associate at a regional distribution warehouse, which requires prolonged standing, moderate lifting, and occasional work in cooler storage areas. These environmental factors, combined with irregular breaks for hydration, tend to precipitate or worsen his vaso-occlusive pain episodes.

PAIN AND VOC HISTORY: He describes his baseline pain as low-grade (2–3/10) in his lower back and hips, with superimposed VOC episodes several times per month that can escalate to 8–9/10, often involving his lower back, thighs, and chest. Historically, he has tried to manage milder pain at home with scheduled acetaminophen and ibuprofen, along with prescribed oral hydromorphone for breakthrough pain. However, in the past year he has required multiple ED visits when home measures were insufficient. He reports that early IV hydration and prompt IV opioid administration can sometimes prevent full admission, but delays in care frequently lead to longer ED stays and hospitalizations.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS (per referring hematologist and patient report):
- Hydroxyurea 1,500 mg by mouth once daily (on stable dose for ~18 months, with prior titration based on ANC and MCV). 
- Folic acid 1 mg by mouth once daily.
- Hydromorphone 4 mg tablets by mouth every 4–6 hours as needed for severe pain; typically uses 1–2 tablets on 2–3 days per week during flares.
- Acetaminophen 500 mg by mouth every 6 hours as needed for mild-to-moderate pain (kept under maximum daily dose).
- Ibuprofen 600 mg by mouth every 8 hours as needed for pain, taken with food to reduce GI upset.
- Cetirizine 10 mg by mouth once daily as needed during allergy seasons.
- Vitamin D3 2,000 IU by mouth once daily.

SOCIAL AND SUPPORT CONTEXT: Marcus lives with his older sister, who provides transportation for medical appointments when he is not feeling well. He has reliable access to a car but prefers morning appointments to avoid rush-hour traffic and to minimize time away from work. He has employer-sponsored insurance and is highly motivated to maintain consistent employment; however, he has already used most of his sick days this year due to VOCs and hospitalizations. He has expressed anxiety about losing his job or benefits if his health does not stabilize.

INFUSION PREFERENCES AND PRIOR IV EXPERIENCE: Marcus has had numerous IV placements for hospitalizations and ED visits and reports that he has moderately difficult peripheral access, especially when dehydrated or in severe pain. He strongly prefers experienced IV nurses and requests that ultrasound-guided IV placement be considered if initial attempts are challenging. He has tolerated prior IV medications and fluids well, with no history of anaphylaxis or severe infusion reactions. He does experience mild anxiety around needles and appreciates clear explanations before each step and the option to use distraction techniques (music, phone, conversation) during IV placement and infusion.

SCHEDULING AND LOGISTICS: He prefers ADAKVEO infusions to be scheduled on Friday mornings around 8:00–9:00 a.m. when possible, as his employer is more flexible at the end of the week and he can use the weekend to recover if he feels fatigued after infusion. He is generally punctual and has reliable transportation but should be contacted at least 48 hours in advance for any scheduling changes. Primary contact is via mobile phone; voicemail is set up and regularly checked, and he also responds promptly to email for appointment reminders.

OTHER CLINICAL NOTES: No history of stroke or acute chest syndrome in the last 5 years, but he had one episode of acute chest syndrome as a teenager. Mild chronic anemia at baseline (Hgb typically 8–9 g/dL). Last documented vaccination review (2025-09) showed he is up to date on influenza and pneumococcal vaccines. He denies current tobacco use and reports only occasional alcohol consumption (1–2 drinks per month). No illicit drug use. He is open to counseling and social work support if needed to help manage chronic disease stress and employment-related concerns.

SPECIAL INSTRUCTIONS FOR INFUSION CENTER: 
- Please verify pain level on arrival and coordinate with his existing outpatient pain plan if he presents in active VOC on infusion day; contact ordering physician before proceeding if he appears acutely ill or unstable.
- Encourage oral hydration before and after infusion; offer warm blankets to reduce cold-triggered pain.
- Monitor closely during first and second infusions for any signs of hypersensitivity or infusion-related reactions (e.g., fever, chills, chest tightness, dyspnea, rash). Follow standard ADAKVEO infusion reaction protocol and notify ordering physician immediately if concerning symptoms develop.
- Provide Marcus with written information about ADAKVEO, expected benefits, potential side effects, and contact numbers for after-hours concerns, as he is very engaged in understanding his treatment plan and likes to share details with his family.

Overall, goal of therapy is to reduce the frequency and severity of VOCs, decrease ED visits and hospitalizations, preserve his ability to maintain employment, and improve his day-to-day quality of life.
